data.title: How Singaporean pro boxer uses skills from the ring to dominate life’s other fights
data.description:
Efasha Kamarudin is not easily pushed around. As a child, she took up karate on the advice of her parents, who wanted her to be able to defend herself if she was ever bullied or attacked.
Kamarudin, or “Fash” as she is known to friends, enjoyed karate so much that she later enrolled in Muay Thai and Brazilian jiu-jitsu classes. She liked that these combat sports required focus, discipline and resilience. As a teenager, she also played football and was a rock climber.
Today, the 34-year-old professional boxer from Singapore feels at home in the boxing ring. She is a tough competitor – staying composed under pressure, standing her ground and sticking to her game plan when things get scrappy.
She worked hard to get to this point.
In 2016, Kamarudin, whose fight name is “The Face”, became the first Singaporean woman to win an international boxing gold medal. It was at the Hong Kong City Cup, an annual boxing competition hosted by the Hong Kong Boxing Association, now known as The Boxing Association of Hong Kong, China.

In mid-2025, she was ranked 15th in the world by the World Boxing Council in the super flyweight division, whose competitors weigh 51kg (112.4lb) to 52.2kg.
She is currently working her way up the ranks and hopes to take the top spot well before her 40th birthday.
Kamarudin’s entry into boxing started over a decade ago, when she injured her foot playing football, forcing her to take time out. She moved her training to the gym, where an instructor suggested that she try boxing. He later saw that she had talent and asked her to take part in a match.
“The then president of the Singapore Boxing Federation noticed my performance and invited me to join the national team. They were preparing for the 2015 Southeast Asian Games and were trying to put a squad together,” Kamarudin says.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Fash The Face (@fash_theface)



She decided to take the sport more seriously, even though she worked full time as a preschool teacher and could only train after work.
Kamarudin boxed regularly over the next few years. A boxing promoter suggested she turn professional. Her then boyfriend – now husband – Russell Harrison encouraged her to do so. She turned pro in 2018, at the age of 27.
“Russell has been instrumental to my career as a boxer,” says Kamarudin, who met the Australian in 2016. He is CEO of Spartans Boxing Club in Singapore and her coach.
“I’ve developed so much under his guidance,” she adds.
In 2025, she fought in five competitions, which she says is “a lot”. Elite professional boxers typically take on only two or three fights a year due to the amount of time required for training and recovery before and after.
She has won nine of 11 bouts in her career, with one draw and one loss. In August 2025, she was defeated by Australian competitor Jasmine Parr, daughter of combat sports legend John Wayne Parr. She had a bounce-back win against French boxer Loetizia Campana in Thailand in November.
“I want to make it to the top level, so I’m doing as many fights as I can,” she says.

Kamarudin and Harrison, who married in 2021, have a daughter together. Zahra turns two this year.
It was not until she became a mother that Kamarudin realised how much of an impact boxing has had on her life.
“I was depressed for a few weeks after giving birth, but I knew it was normal because of the hormonal and other physical changes I was going through,” she says.
“But instead of confining myself to the house and avoiding physical activity, like many new mums in Asia do, I decided to go back to the gym.
“I didn’t work out intensely at first. I trained a little here and there, and pretty soon I was back in the ring. It felt great having this outlet for all the emotions I was experiencing.
“It also reconnected me with myself. After having my daughter, I felt like I’d lost my sense of individuality, and returning to boxing helped me overcome that.”

Research shows that boxing can help improve mental health and emotional well-being in a number of ways. A review published in 2022 in the American Journal of Lifestyle Medicine found that non-contact boxing is a healthy outlet for anger and aggression, lowers stress, increases self-esteem, improves quality of life, and reduces symptoms of conditions like anxiety and depression.
A 2023 study revealed that the sport encourages psychological resilience – the ability to “bounce back” from adversity or stress and adapt well to challenges.
Kamarudin can vouch for the transformative power of boxing. Besides helping her get through what she calls a “dark period” after having her baby, she says that the sport has, over the years, worked wonders on her self-confidence.
“It’s helped me with my body-image issues. I’ve always had an athletic build, and I used to compare myself to my younger sister, who was skinny. I also used to restrict my carb intake, thinking that carbs were fattening,” she says.
“Boxing reminds me that I’m not big or chunky at all, just muscular, and being muscular is a good thing because it means I’m strong. It’s really changed how I see myself. It’s made me appreciate my body more.”

She would like to see more young women take up boxing as it offers an excellent total-body workout. But it is more than just a way to keep fit or lose weight. It can help participants feel more empowered.
“Many people start boxing because they want to learn a new skill or relieve stress. Whatever the reason, it can make you feel better about yourself. When you train and see results, you feel like you can do just about anything.”
For women who are concerned about boxing being dangerous, Kamarudin says it is important to select the right gym, one in which you feel safe and that follows safety precautions, so you do not end up bruised or injured. Joining a gym with many female members may help create a sense of belonging.
Kamarudin currently juggles training and motherhood, something that she admits is “hard and exhausting”, especially when she is preparing for a competition.
She also co-owns a business with a friend. Launched in 2022, BXHR (pronounced “box-her”) specialises in boxing gloves for women.
Kamarudin’s ultimate goal is to be an “undisputed champion”, which would entail holding major boxing belts from the sport’s four governing bodies: the WBA (World Boxing Association), the WBC (World Boxing Council), the IBF (International Boxing Federation) and the WBO (World Boxing Organisation).
“I have none right now, but I’m giving myself four more years of doing this professionally until I hold all four belts,” she says confidently.
“I believe that I can get there.”
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: Mainland Chinese money to drive 10% rise in Hong Kong commercial property deals: Colliers
data.description:
Surging mainland Chinese investment in Hong Kong’s commercial real estate sector has helped set the stage for a “measured recovery” in 2026, according to Colliers.
In the luxury residential segment, mainland capital accounted for about 80 per cent of transactions exceeding HK$100 million on The Peak and in Southern district, according to Centaline Property Agency.
Investment from the mainland rose to the highest level in five years in the last quarter of 2025, and deal value in 2026 was set to increase by 10 per cent, Colliers said.
In the last three months of last year, capital from the mainland accounted for about 60 per cent of the sector’s “big-ticket deals”, defined as those with a value of more than HK$100 million (US$12.8 million), the property consultancy said in a report on Wednesday.
Last year “marked a turning point” for the city’s battered commercial property sector, with total investment value jumping 12 per cent from a year earlier to HK$39 billion, said Thomas Chak, head of capital markets and investment services at Colliers Hong Kong.
“This momentum sets the stage for 2026, where we expect capital deployment to accelerate as investors move to capture repricing opportunities before they narrow,” Chak said.
International investors accounted for 15 per cent of fourth-quarter investment, with the rest coming from Hong Kong-based investors, Colliers said. For the full year, there were 85 major property deals, marginally higher than 2024’s 82, it added.
Office assets in prime central business district locations remained undervalued, while other assets continued to offer strong fundamentals and medium to long-term growth potential, Chak said.
“With Chinese mainland capital and global funds returning, and full-year transaction value forecast to climb up to 10 per cent, Hong Kong is poised for a measured recovery supported by diversified investment demand,” he said.
Colliers estimated that 2026 investment in Hong Kong’s commercial real estate could reach HK$42 billion.
The biggest deal last year was also the biggest since 2021: Alibaba Group Holding and Ant Group shelled out HK$7.2 billion in October to buy the top floors of One Causeway Bay, the office tower that Mandarin Oriental International is building on the site of The Excelsior hotel.

The acquisition totalled 301,555 sq ft, comprising the top 13 floors of the 24-storey building, with parking space for 50 vehicles and signage rights to the tower. Alibaba owns the Post.
Meanwhile, OCBC Hong Kong, a unit of Singapore-headquartered lender OCBC, bought China Huarong Tower, a 26-storey office tower in Wan Chai, in the fourth quarter for HK$1.2 billion, according to information tracked by Colliers.
“Chinese mainland capital is flowing back into the Hong Kong market, and investors from Southeast Asia and international funds are also returning, viewing the city as a market for diversification and growth in selected sectors,” the report said.
As for investment picks, Colliers said the office and accommodation sectors were favoured, as the former presented “undervalued opportunities”, while the latter rested on “solid fundamentals” and offered medium to long-term growth.
The residential segment was also poised to remain an “ultra-luxury property year”, with new and secondary luxury home deals likely to jump by 50 per cent and 60 per cent, respectively, according to Centaline. Prices were estimated to rise by as much as 10 per cent.
The bet on upscale homes was underpinned by geopolitical uncertainties, which had prompted wealthy mainland Chinese buyers to favour Hong Kong’s trophy assets.
In addition, the city’s various talent admission schemes had welcomed about 260,000 professionals and high-net-worth individuals and their families as of mid-December, the property agency said. These families were expected to purchase high-end properties in Hong Kong as they made the city their base, it said.
Hong Kong’s initial public offering fundraising was also projected to hit HK$350 billion this year, with about 150 new stocks making their debut, which could translate to more allocation for luxury properties, Centaline said.

data.title: South Korea’s Yoon could become ‘martyr’ if given death penalty: observers
data.description:
When prosecutors in South Korea demanded the death penalty for ex-president Yoon Suk-yeol, he shook his head in disbelief and let out a contemptuous laugh – a moment that encapsulated his defiant posture throughout nine months of hearings.
Yoon stands accused of being the “ringleader” of an insurrection stemming from his failed martial law bid in 2024 that plunged the country into political chaos.
The public gallery, packed with Yoon’s supporters, erupted into protests, forcing the presiding judge to repeatedly call for order at the final hearing on Tuesday, which stretched 17 hours and past midnight.
A three-judge panel is set to deliver its verdict and sentence on the 65-year-old on February 19, weighing the prosecution’s request and any mitigating factors.
Special prosecutor Cho Eun-suk’s team sought the death penalty, accusing Yoon of orchestrating an “insurrection” by imposing martial law in a bid to cling to power through the seizure of the judiciary and legislature.
Prosecutors also demanded life imprisonment for former defence minister Kim Yong-hyun and a 30-year prison term for retired army intelligence chief No Sang-won, accused of designing the martial law plan.
“This was a crime of insurrection aimed at neutralising constitutional state bodies to extend power through military and police forces,” the special counsel said.


Professor Lim Ji-bong of Sogang University Law School said the death penalty request was widely anticipated, as Yoon was charged as a ringleader of an insurrection – an offence punishable by either death or life imprisonment under South Korean criminal law if convicted.
“We all witnessed troops flown by helicopter storming the National Assembly, smashing windows. That alone constitutes insurrection by obstructing the legislature’s constitutional function,” Lim told This Week in Asia.
However, Lim said the court was more likely to impose life imprisonment, noting that a death sentence could “turn Yoon into a martyr among his supporters and further inflame political divisions”.
South Korea has not carried out an execution since 1997 and Amnesty International classifies the country as abolitionist in practice.
“There are few mitigating factors for Yoon,” Lim said. “He has shown no genuine remorse, mocked the prosecution, justified martial law with sophistry and encouraged his lawyers to delay proceedings.”
Prosecutors further accused Yoon of attempting to provoke a military response from North Korea by ordering drone incursions in October and November 2024, allegedly to manufacture a pretext for declaring martial law.
They argued the failed decree gravely damaged the political neutrality of the military and police, deepened social polarisation and undermined the economy and public trust.

In his final statement, Yoon – a former prosecutor general – dismissed the case as a collection of “fantasies and fictions”, insisting his declaration of martial law was meant to awaken the public to what he called the “tyranny” of a National Assembly controlled by the then liberal opposition Democratic Party of Korea (DPK).
He likened the special prosecutors to “a pack of wolves blindly rushing in”, accusing them of serving “dark forces” allegedly embedded within the DPK. He also accused the opposition of collaborating with vague “anti-state, subversive and external forces” to manipulate public opinion and sow discord.
“They carried out an unconstitutional dictatorship of the National Assembly, paralysed state affairs and drove the country to the brink of ruin,” Yoon said.
“I declared martial law to inform the people of this national crisis and seek their cooperation.”
Yoon’s lawyers insisted that the declaration of martial law fell under the president’s exclusive authority and was therefore beyond judicial review.
They said Yoon had considered alternatives – including a national referendum and lawsuits to dissolve unconstitutional political parties – but ultimately chose martial law as “the least disruptive” means of alerting the public.
His statement marked the end of a trial marred by repeated controversies over the court’s perceived failure to rein in defence tactics widely criticised as procedural filibustering.
During a recent hearing, prosecutors asked a lawyer for co-defendant Kim to speed up after he read his statement at an exceptionally slow pace.
“I can’t speak fast because of my short tongue,” the lawyer quipped, prompting laughter in the courtroom.

Professor Lee Jae-mook of Hankuk University of Foreign Studies said Yoon’s “remorseless and scornful” courtroom demeanour only intensified public anger.
“Beyond legal responsibility, he refuses to accept political responsibility for the social chaos and economic damage caused by martial law,” Lee told This Week in Asia.
“His conduct borders on the irrational – displaying contempt, mockery and a striking lack of empathy like a psychopath.”
The centrist Hankook Ilbo newspaper wrote in an editorial that Yoon consistently evaded responsibility through sophistry, even shifting blame onto his ministers by claiming that “not a single cabinet member warned of political backlash”.
The paper criticised Yoon’s defence for invoking Enlightenment philosopher Montesquieu – an advocate of separation of powers – to argue that martial law lay beyond judicial scrutiny.
Given the gravity of the charges and Yoon’s lack of remorse, it said, a severe sentence was necessary to send a clear message: “Even the most powerful individual cannot seize authority not granted by the people.”
The conservative Chosun newspaper described martial law as a “relic of authoritarian rule”, noting that Yoon’s decree and subsequent impeachment shocked the nation and damaged its dignity.
“Even after prosecutors sought the death penalty, Yoon has offered no sincere apology or remorse,” it said. “This is deeply troubling.”
The paper urged political leaders to learn from the crisis, warning that relentless partisan conflict continued to overshadow that lesson.


The progressive Hankyoreh accused Yoon of pursuing emergency powers from the start of his presidency in 2022 to eliminate political opposition and monopolise power.
It alleged that the martial law decree was partly intended to shield his wife from bribery and influence-peddling scandals – charges that led to her arrest after Yoon’s impeachment.
“For a people who overcame brutal military dictatorship through pro-democracy uprisings, Yoon Suk-yeol’s coup attempt was intolerable,” the paper said, praising citizens for swiftly suppressing the insurrection.
It also criticised the judiciary, citing an earlier decision by a panel led by senior judge Ji Gwi-yeon that temporarily freed Yoon on procedural grounds.
“The court must not betray the public’s demand for a stern sentence if democracy is to take root,” it said.
This marks the first time in 30 years that prosecutors have sought the death penalty for an ex-president.
The last case involved former authoritarian leader Chun Doo-hwan, who was sentenced to death for the 1979 military coup and the 1980 Gwangju massacre.
His sentence was later commuted to life imprisonment before he was pardoned in 1997.

data.title: Hong Kong’s John Lee to open new Legco’s first meeting with Tai Po fire on agenda
data.description:
Lawmakers for the latest Legco term were elected on December 7 last year, 11 days after the deadly fire broke out in Tai Po.
It was the second Legco election held under Beijing’s “patriots-only” electoral overhaul. The turnout rate in the geographical constituency races saw a slight rebound to 31.9 per cent, up from the record low of 30.2 per cent in 2021.
Of the 90 lawmakers, 20 were directly elected by residents from geographical constituencies, while 30 and 40 came from the functional and Election Committee constituencies, where members were chosen by their respective small circles.

data.title: Trump flips off factory worker after Epstein taunt
data.description:
US President Donald Trump raised his ‌middle finger and appeared to direct profanity towards a factory worker who expressed ‍criticism of his handling of the Jeffrey Epstein controversy at a Michigan auto plant on Tuesday, video circulating online showed.
The entertainment site TMZ first published video capturing the exchange, and the White House did not dispute its authenticity.
“A lunatic was ⁠wildly screaming expletives in a complete fit of rage, and the President gave an appropriate and unambiguous response,” White House spokesman Steven Cheung said in an email.
Trump was touring the Ford F-150 assembly facility in Dearborn when a worker on the plant floor shouted what sounded like “paedophile protector” as the ‍president stood on an elevated walkway, the video showed.

Trump turned towards the individual and appeared to respond ‍with an expletive before making a hand gesture with his middle finger as he walked off.
Other Ford employees cheered and welcomed the president as he toured ‌the assembly line, taking photos with workers and shaking hands.
Ford Executive Chairman Bill Ford, speaking with the media at ‍an event later, called the incident unfortunate and said he was embarrassed by it.
“That was six seconds out of an hour tour. And the tour went great,” he said. “I think he really enjoyed it and we did too.”
Trump has occasionally used ‌profanity in ⁠public settings, often in response to criticism, confrontation or to emphasise his point.
Trump has faced persistent scrutiny for his handling of sensitive federal records tied to Epstein, a convicted sex offender who died by suicide in jail in 2019.
Many of Trump’s most loyal supporters believe the government is withholding documents that ‌would reveal the late financier’s ties to powerful public figures. Trump has repeatedly denied any knowledge of Epstein’s alleged abuse and sex trafficking of girls and has not been accused ‌of wrongdoing.
Trump’s visit to ‌the Michigan facility was part of an effort to highlight his administration’s support for US manufacturing, a key theme as the 2026 election year unfolds. Michigan is seen as a politically important state, and domestic jobs and industry have been central to Trump’s economic messaging.

data.title: Can Singapore’s farming future grow in Malaysia’s US$123 million agriculture hub?
data.description:
Vincent Wei led fellow Singaporean farmers around an empty Malaysian plot, laying out plans for a greenhouse and rows of leafy vegetables. What he pitched was not just space for crops but a lifeline for growers struggling to make ends meet in a city state with high prices and little vacant land.
The to-be agriculture hub is part of a joint special economic zone launched last year by the two neighbours, expected to cost US$123 million and produce 10,000 tonnes of fresh produce annually. It is luring Singapore’s farmers with promises of cheaper land, labour and energy just over the border.
Wei is confident of securing farmers for the 80-hectare (200-acre) site, promising they can “reduce their cost of set-up and operations” there. Representing a joint venture between his agritech firm Archisen and Southern Catalyst backed by Malaysia’s Ministry of Finance, he aims to get them going by the third quarter of this year, offering 25-year land leases bundled with infrastructure and electricity.
“It’s an aggressive timeline,” Wei admitted, as earth diggers roamed the plot clearing it of palm trees. But wait any longer and some of those ventures may already be out of business, he said.
Singapore, a country smaller than New York City, has tried in recent years to reinvigorate its farming industry, which over decades has been crowded out by skyscrapers and housing blocks. Just this month, it opened the world’s tallest vertical farm valued at S$80 million (US$62 million) and set to produce up to 2,000 tonnes of vegetables like lettuce and spinach a year.
Yet, a string of farm and start-up closures has underlined how hi-tech agriculture is far from being a definitive solution for the island state’s food security.


The fledgling sector has been buffeted by “headwinds – supply chain disruptions, inflationary pressures on energy and manpower costs, and a tougher financing environment”, Sustainability and Environment Minister Grace Fu said in November.
That has forced the government to scale back an ambitious plan to meet a third of its population’s nutritional needs through local production by the end of this decade. Still keen to protect against disruptions like those seen during the Covid-19 pandemic and Russia-Ukraine war, the country’s new target is to meet 20 per cent of fibre and 30 per cent of protein needs locally by 2035.
While Singapore has poured more than S$300 million into agritech research and development, some farmers complain that it does little to ease the cost pressures of actually running a farm. Grace Lim, co-founder of a vertical farm GroGrace, has been losing about S$25,000 a month since her electricity bill doubled between 2019 and 2025. She called for the government to subsidise the sector, like in the European Union, which is expanding payments and simplifying financing for farmers.
“Why are they letting perfectly working commercial farms die while they continue to pour money into research that doesn’t actually grow your real food?” she said.
The mounting losses have also frightened off investors. Private funding for agriculture ventures in Singapore peaked at US$1.1 billion in 2021 but had dwindled to just a 10th of that by last year, according to venture capital firm AgFunder.
Wei believes the industry is in a “fundraising winter”, where start-ups are hard-pressed to find investors because they are unable to turn a profit.

Enter the Johor-Singapore special economic zone where Wei, his clients and other firms are starting to shift or expand their operations.
Singapore-based Vegeponics, for instance, is expanding its container and vertical farms to the SEZ. By having facilities in both countries, the vegetable grower expects to cut operational costs – which can reach S$70,000 a month – by about a third, marketing manager Jesper Fan said.
The move will also be welcome for retailers and customers seeking price and supply stability. Singapore is one of the world’s most expensive countries where essentials like milk and eggs are flown in from as far away as Australia and Poland, resulting in eye-watering grocery bills.
But what would be a boost to farmers’ bottom lines may end up deepening the island nation’s dependence on food imports. Singaporean growers who dream of building out their local industry to bolster the country’s food security will have to get used to growing their produce on foreign soil.
Wei said the agriculture hub in the SEZ could nonetheless support closer cross-border cooperation and strengthen regional food resilience in Southeast Asia and further afield as Japanese, South Korean and Chinese farmers also show interest.
For Wei, what began as investor pitches and site walks around overgrown fields is gradually giving way to discussions on farm layouts and investment timelines.
“I think it is high time something like this is done,” he said. “If you don’t work with your neighbour, who do you work with?”

data.title: Hong Kong stocks edge higher as investors hedge against Middle East uncertainty
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks rose on Wednesday as investors diversified away from US assets amid rising tensions between the US and Iran.
The Hang Seng Index advanced 0.2 per cent to 26,892.67 as of 9.55am, adding to a 0.9 per cent gain on Tuesday. The benchmark briefly touched 27,143 points on Tuesday, its highest since last November. The Hang Seng Tech Index added 0.6 per cent.
On the mainland, the CSI 300 Index rose 0.7 per cent and the Shanghai Composite Index climbed 0.4 per cent.
Leading the gainers, e-commerce firm Alibaba Group Holding jumped 2.6 per cent to HK$164.10 and smartphone and carmaker Xiaomi advanced 1.3 per cent to HK$38.46. Short-video platform Kuaishou Technology rose 4.1 per cent to HK$81.70, its highest since last October, after announcing its video-generation AI product had reached US$20 million in monthly revenue in December.
Limiting gains, online travel-booking agency Trip.com fell 4.7 per cent to HK$581 while food-delivery service provider Meituan slipped 1.6 per cent to HK$103.20, and e-commerce and logistics giant JD.com lost 1.3 per cent to HK$116.
Asian markets found support as geopolitical uncertainty in the Middle East reinforced demand for non-US equities, with some investors viewing Asia as a relative hedge.
Overnight in the US markets, the Dow Jones fell 0.8 per cent while the S&P 500 Index slid 0.2 per cent from its all-time high. Sentiment was partly weighed down by uncertainty over whether the top US court would move against tariffs that were announced in April. An unfavourable ruling on Wednesday could trigger a negative market reaction.
Elsewhere in the Asia-Pacific region, Japan’s Nikkei 225 climbed 1.4 per cent while South Korea’s Kospi and Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 lost 0.2 per cent.

data.title: India proposes reopening government bidding to Chinese firms as ties thaw
data.description:
India plans to lift long-standing restrictions on Chinese companies bidding for government contracts, a move officials say is aimed at easing shortages and project delays as relations with Beijing show tentative signs of stabilising.
The proposal, first reported by Reuters, would roll back curbs, imposed after the two countries’ deadly 2020 border clash in the Galwan Valley, that effectively shut Chinese firms out of a vast segment of India’s public procurement market.
In October 2024, India and China completed a significant phase of troop disengagement in eastern Ladakh and agreed on coordinated patrolling arrangements, easing tensions along the disputed frontier and creating political space for limited economic re-engagement.
Srividya Jandhyala, an associate professor of management at the ESSEC Business School, Asia-Pacific, said the proposed easing came at a time of “cautious rebuilding” of ties between the two countries.

“Re-establishing economic ties with China could potentially drive new foreign investment and access to Chinese technology. This is especially relevant at a time when India has seen a sharp decline in foreign direct investment flows,” Jandhyala told This Week in Asia.
Under current rules, Chinese companies must register with a designated Indian government committee and obtain political and security clearances before they can bid for public contracts. They are also blocked from bidding for major government contracts, estimated at US$700 billion to US$750 billion.
This has had significant impact, particularly in infrastructure and manufacturing. A 2024 report by the Observer Research Foundation showed the value of new projects awarded to Chinese bidders fell 26.9 per cent from a year earlier to US$1.67 billion in 2021.
Need for assurance
The finance ministry plans to ease the rules after other departments complained of shortages and delays.
However, Jandhyala cautioned against expecting Chinese firms to return in droves.
“Following years of challenges in India – and in the West – Chinese firms have already diversified to other markets in Asia and around the world. It is not an inevitable outcome that they will prioritise investment in India,” Jandhyala said.
Even if policy restrictions were removed, Chinese firms would still be looking for additional guarantees of trust, market opportunities and investment security, she added.
In March 2025, Beijing called for closer trade cooperation with New Delhi to counter growing US tariffs on Chinese and Indian goods. “Chinese companies would like to be assured that India’s relationship with China would improve even if a trade deal with the US is finalised,” Jandhyala said.


Natasha Agarwal, co-founder of The Global South Convergence Forum, a research-based forum in Mumbai, said that the benefit to the local economy would depend on three variables: what restrictions would be removed, India’s capacity to benefit from Chinese investments, and the nature and extent of these investments.
“China has clearly established its role in global politics. Concerning India, Beijing will operate from a space where it deeply understands Delhi’s equation with the US, and thus its potential impact on China,” she said.
“China is going to give what it wants to give and at the cost price, financial and non-financial. India has done little and continues to do little when it comes to easing concerns from China. Businesses cannot operate in such an uncertain environment.”
In October last year, India and China resumed direct flights after a five-year suspension, while in December, Delhi freed up visas for Chinese professionals – both steps towards rebuilding the relationship between the two countries.
‘Calibrated pragmatism’
Saheli Chattaraj, an assistant professor of Chinese studies at Somaiya Vidyavihar University, said that easing restrictions on Chinese companies might not necessarily imply a reversal of India’s post-Galwan strategic posture.
“Instead, it can be interpreted as a shift from blanket exclusion to selective, conditional engagement,” she said. “For the Indian economy, this approach offers practical benefits while retaining policy control, especially when aligned with Made in India objectives.”
Chattaraj said Chinese firms remained among the most cost-competitive suppliers of industrial equipment – particularly in sectors critical to India’s manufacturing push, such as power equipment, heavy machinery, rail and metro components, electronics and electrical hardware.
“Post-2020 restrictions led to supply bottlenecks and project delays, increasing costs for Indian manufacturers and infrastructure developers. Easing these curbs can reduce input costs for Indian firms, improve project timelines and restore predictability in supply chains,” she said.

In July 2024, an Economic Survey report tabled by the finance minister in parliament urged more foreign direct investment from China to enhance India’s exports to the US and other Western countries, and reduce the trade deficit with Beijing.
Chattaraj said a selective easing of restrictions, combined with Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s Shanghai Cooperation Organisation participation, would signal an intent to stabilise and manage relations with China, particularly economically.
“It reflects a recognition in Delhi that prolonged commercial disengagement carries economic costs: infrastructure delays and supply shortages. A total decoupling from China is neither feasible nor desirable in the short to medium term. This indicates a shift from a confrontation-driven China policy to calibrated pragmatism,” she said.
Subsequent Economic Survey reports showed the need for Chinese capital and technology for developing manufacturing in India, said Antara Ghosal Singh, a fellow at the Strategic Studies Programme at the Observer Research Foundation.
“On the other hand, the uncertainty unleashed by Trump 2.0 is causing every country to hedge their bets,” Singh said.
“For China and India, the border agreement reached in October 2024 proved to be a turning point. Back-to-back high-level visits, restarting of Kailash Mansarovar Yatra, restarting of direct flights and now elimination of government bidding restrictions on Chinese companies mean that China-India ties are indeed moving towards normalisation.”

data.title: DeepSeek stays mum on next AI model release as technical papers show frontier innovation
data.description:
Chinese artificial intelligence firm DeepSeek continues to keep the world guessing on when its next major release – the much-anticipated updates to its V3 and R1 models – will be launched, according to analysts, amid its recent publication of technical papers.
The papers underscored DeepSeek’s efforts to improve the underlying infrastructure of AI systems in China at a time when geopolitical tensions and domestic production hurdles restricted the country’s access to advanced semiconductors to train new models, according to Zhang Ruiwang, a Beijing-based information systems architect working in the internet sector.
“DeepSeek just wants to prove that AI infrastructure innovation would drive efficiency and further scale up performance of models,” Zhang said.
Still, speculation swirled across the global AI community about potential delays in the Hangzhou-based start-up’s launch of its next-generation V4 and R2 models, which would succeed V3, introduced in December 2024, and R1, which was released in January last year.
DeepSeek declined to comment on reports that V4 would be released during the Lunar New Year.
Apart from US restrictions on China’s access to cutting-edge graphics processing units (GPUs) and advanced chipmaking equipment, the world’s AI developers currently face a shortage of memory chips amid high demand for these semiconductors in enterprise data centres.

DeepSeek on Tuesday published a new technical paper that proposed a novel technique to train models, which researchers said could facilitate “aggressive parameter expansion” by bypassing GPU memory constraints.
This paper introduced a “conditional memory” technique, called Engram, to address a key bottleneck of scaling up AI models: the limited capacity of GPU high-bandwidth memory.
That followed DeepSeek’s release of an updated technical paper about R1, which featured the contributions of its 18 core scientists.
The paper suggested that DeepSeek retained all 18 scientists behind its AI model development efforts, as well as many of the R1 project’s 176 contributors, despite fierce competition for talent in China’s AI industry.
On the last day of 2025, DeepSeek published a technical paper, with founder and CEO Liang Wenfeng among the 19 co-authors, about “manifold-constrained hyper-connections” (mHC) – a general framework for training AI systems at scale, which suggested “promising directions for the evolution of foundational models”.
The mHC method forms part of DeepSeek’s push to make its AI models more cost-effective, as it strives to keep pace with better-funded US rivals with greater access to advanced computing power.
According to Zhang, these DeepSeek innovations could be a boon for Chinese developers with limited computing and capital resources, as the company focused on going back to the basics and improving these processes.
The firm’s website listed eight foundational models, including new V3 versions – V3.2 and V3.2-Speciale – that were launched last month.
If DeepSeek released next-generation models, either V4 or R2, around the Lunar New Year, Zhang said that the company was not expected to stun the world like it did last year.
“What would be more important was that a Chinese company delivered new breakthroughs to bring AI to the hands of everyone,” Zhang said.
When British journal Nature last month profiled DeepSeek’s Liang as part of its top 10 “people who shaped science in 2025”, it recognised the disruption caused by R1’s release in January last year, as the Chinese start-up showed that “the United States was not as far ahead in AI as many experts had thought”.

data.title: The Chinese century may already be here
data.description:
Some people wonder whether China could carry out a sophisticated state-sponsored kidnapping like the US capture of Venezuelan president Nicolas Maduro and his wife. And it’s not just that; there is state terrorism like remote assassinations by drones, which also end up killing a lot of civilian bystanders, not to mention breaching the sovereignty of their countries. The United States can apparently do that anywhere around the world.
Such Hollywood-style cowboy militarism no doubt makes its leaders feel all-powerful. That’s the problem. If you are a hammer, everything looks like a nail. That completely warps your sense of reality. But when violence becomes your first or primary resort rather than your last, you end up hurting not only others but yourself as well. That has pretty much been the recent history of US foreign policy.
“We’re re-establishing deterrence that’s so absolute and so unquestioned that our enemies will not dare to test us,” declared US Secretary of Defence Pete Hegseth after the raid in Caracas.
I, for one, hope China will not need to develop capabilities to kidnap or kill other leaders at such a sophisticated hi-tech military level. A sense of constraint and limitation is what a state or an individual needs to keep it real.
After all, what purpose would it serve to, say, kidnap or kill Taiwanese leader William Lai Ching-te? That wouldn’t bring unification any closer. In fact, it would vastly complicate a combustible situation.
Sorry, but Beijing is far too smart for that, much smarter than some commentators whose default mode of China analysis seems to be what may be called “monkey see, monkey do”. Russian President Vladimir Putin invaded Ukraine, so wouldn’t President Xi Jinping follow suit with Taiwan? US President Donald Trump kidnapped Maduro, so won’t Xi try the same with Lai?


That’s not Chinese statecraft. State-organised violence is a means, not the goal of statecraft.
Successfully or not, China’s leaders have for centuries been taught to ask the right questions to get the big picture. For today’s leaders, it’s imperative to be doing that if they and their country hope to survive and prosper in the 21st century.
But don’t just take that from me.
“Beijing appears to recognize that in statecraft, focusing one’s energies on the right question matters,” said Rush Doshi, who was deputy senior director for China and Taiwan in the National Security Council during the Joe Biden administration.
One of five specialists surveyed by The New York Times about their prognoses for the world, he observes that China’s economy is already about 30 per cent larger than that of the United States when measured by purchasing power, its industrial base is twice as big and its power generation twice as high.


China also leads in new tech industries such as electric vehicles and next-generation nuclear reactors while the US has become heavily dependent on Chinese supply chains from antibiotics to rare earths.
“For Washington, the central strategic question of the 21st century is not whether the United States can build a bastion in the Western Hemisphere,” Doshi said. “It is whether America, after a century as the world’s most powerful, advanced and prosperous country, will renew the true sources of strength or pass the torch to China.”
Another in The New York Times series was Adam Tooze, a Columbia University historian. “If global competition is inextricably interwoven with technology and energy, how states power themselves could predict how the next world order takes shape,” he said.
“Today, China is a classic example of a power state. It pursues energy in every direction, harnessing an army of scientists and industrial R&D,” he said, also adding that “with the grotesque caricature presented by America today, it is tempting to paint China as a haven of light: clear waters and green mountains, as President Xi Jinping likes to say”.


There are other tech advances that may be just as crucial. China has developed unrivalled deep-seabed prospecting and mining capabilities. “Xi has targeted the deep seabed as a priority area for Chinese leadership,” wrote Elizabeth Economy, a senior fellow at Stanford University’s Hoover Institution, in Foreign Affairs.
It is already leading in advanced sensor technology. According to the Australian Strategic Policy Institute, which usually publishes research materials critical of China, Beijing is leading research in nearly 90 per cent of 74 technologies deemed crucial to national interests.
If they are right, it looks like we are entering the Chinese century. But even if we are not – and frankly that’s OK by me – there won’t be another American century.
